The scene transitions to a ceremonial moment on the track, where a line of uniformed musicians in striking red and black ceremonial attire with ornate gold helmets and white gloves play a building brass fanfare on their instruments, including bugles. The camera pans across the packed stadium, showcasing the massive, cheering crowd and vibrant atmosphere, with banners reading 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' and '#BE THE NEXT,' accompanied by audible crowd noise and applause. The final shot focuses on Fabrice Lapierre of Australia, wearing a teal and yellow singlet, standing on the track with a focused expression, his personal best (PB) of 8.40m and season best (SB) of 8.03m displayed on screen, as a commentator announces, '12 athletes in the final of the men's long jump. Let's meet them now.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with commentary introducing Fabrice Lapierre of Australia, a former world indoor champion now 33 years of age, as he walks along the track with a focused expression in his teal and yellow singlet, his season's best of 8.03m displayed on screen. The camera then shifts to Luvo Manyonga of South Africa, described as 'the big man himself' and the Commonwealth record holder with a PB and SB of 8.65m, who raises his arms in celebration in his green and yellow singlet. Next, Maykel Massó of Cuba, an 18-year-old junior who was fourth in the world and Youth Junior Championships, is seen with a determined look, raising his arm in his red singlet, his PB and SB at 8.33m. Jarrion Lawson of the USA, a former sprinter who won the 100m, 200m, long jump, and triple jump in collegiate championships, confidently raises his arm in his blue Nike singlet, with his PB at 8.58m and SB at 8.33m. Ruswahl Samaai, another strong South African contender, smiles broadly and raises his arms in his green and yellow singlet, his PB and SB at 8.49m. Radek Juška of the Czech Republic, a world bronze medalist two years prior who produced the biggest jump in qualifying at 8.24m, raises his arm with a composed expression in his red singlet, his PB and SB at 8.29m. Finally, Jianan Wang of China, highlighted as one of their good athletes, raises his arm with a calm demeanor in his red singlet, his PB and SB also at 8.29m. Throughout, the bright, even lighting of a major evening athletics event illuminates a packed stadium crowd and advertising banners for IAAF.org, TDK, Toyota, and Hydrogen Fuel Cell, while an official with a clipboard is seated in the background, all contributing to the charged atmosphere with continuous crowd noise and enthusiastic commentary.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens at the IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017, capturing Australian long jumper Fabrice Lapierre as he prepares for his attempt. He is seen stretching and removing his teal pants on the red track, wearing his distinctive teal and yellow 'AUSTRALIA' singlet with 'LAPIERRE' and 'LONDON 2017' on his bib. The stadium is brightly lit, with the 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' banner prominently displayed, and a massive, diverse crowd fills the stands, some waving flags and clapping, creating an electric atmosphere. A male commentator immediately focuses on Lapierre, noting that coaches are watching intently and describing him as "really a talent" who "hasn't really produced it in the last two or three years," also mentioning his unique habit of running up with a "chain in his teeth." Lapierre, whose personal best of 8.40m and season best of 8.03m are displayed, then sprints down the track with intense focus, executing a powerful jump into the sandpit. A low-angle shot emphasizes the precision of his foot on the takeoff board. The commentator exclaims, "Oh, that's a good reasonable first jump, close to the not over the 8-meter mark," and later adds, "Good on the board, just behind it." A female official in a white shirt and black pants kneels beside the pit, waving a white flag to signal a valid jump. After landing, Lapierre walks away as the scoreboard displays his result: 7.89 meters with a wind reading of +0.7 m/s, placing him in first position. The commentator, while acknowledging it's "not a bad start" and a "comfortable start," expresses that they "expected to see much bigger than that" in the competition, and analyzes Lapierre's technique, observing "Good height this man. Just lacking a little bit of speed really in his approach to the board," while explaining the competition strategy of needing a good jump in the first three attempts to advance. The general stadium ambiance and crowd noise are present throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Australian long jumper Fabrice Lapierre, wearing a yellow and teal singlet, leaning forward on a barrier, appearing focused. A commentator announces his jump of "7.89," noting it's "just below that 8 meters," and adds there was "a little bit of a headwind." The scene then cuts to Luvo Manyonga of South Africa, in a green and yellow singlet, standing with hands on hips, looking intense and focused. As Manyonga walks down the track, preparing for his attempt with a coach in a red 'CZECH REPUBLIC' hoodie nearby, the commentator exclaims, "Well, here we go. One of the most exciting as man this man is when he gets it right," mentioning a "problem with his run up in the qualifying" but assuring he is "capable of a massive jump." The camera captures Manyonga’s pre-jump ritual – raising his arms, adjusting his headband, and taking deep breaths, with his PB and SB of 8.65m displayed – as the commentator utters "Manyonga. Ooh!" and the crowd reacts with a collective gasp, building tension. The atmosphere is electric, with the dense and engaged crowd visible in wide shots, alongside the 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' banner, as the commentator emphasizes, "This could be a big event for the South Africans really." A second commentator adds, "This could be very, very special, Steve, if it all clicks. Let's see." Manyonga then sprints down the runway, executes a powerful jump, and lands in the sandpit with a distinct thud. A female official in a white shirt and black pants kneels beside the pit and waves a white flag, signaling a valid jump, which prompts the first commentator to excitedly exclaim, "Oh, it's clicked! That's a massive jump!" He then seems to momentarily misinterpret, adding, "But the red flag goes up. Wow!" despite the visual confirmation of a white flag and Manyonga's subsequent celebration. Manyonga walks away from the pit, smiling and celebrating, as officials measure the jump with a long wooden rod, and the commentator notes, "He's laughing. But that was again just showing you what this man can do," concluding, "That was close."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens at the IAAF World Championships London 2017, focusing on the men's long jump event. Damar Forbes of Jamaica, in his yellow 'JAMAICA' singlet with 'FORBES' and 'LONDON 2017' on his bib, prepares for a powerful run-up along the red track, bordered by a 'TOYOTA HYBRID' banner. As he begins, a digital timer on the white and blue takeoff board, marked '8.9', shows '0.0'. Commentators note the stadium crowd giving 'as much encouragement as they can from the stands'. Forbes executes a strong, controlled leap into the sandpit, marked by a distinct thud upon landing near the 8-meter mark. Immediately, a commentator expresses doubt, stating, "Oh, I don't think that's going to do much really." An official in a white shirt and black pants raises a white flag, signaling a valid jump. Forbes, identified by commentators as 'the four-time Jamaican champion', walks away visibly satisfied, later celebrating with a raised arm and a smile, acknowledging the packed stadium under bright lights, with 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' and 'SEIKO' banners visible. The scoreboard displays his previous results of 7.61m and 7.91m, with a wind reading of +0.4. Commentators discuss the disappointment of not performing to potential, mentioning Forbes's lifetime best of 8.29m this year, which "would be good enough to have him on the in a podium position," just ahead of Aleksandr Menkov's 8.27m. They conclude that his latest jump, which they didn't think was "as good as his second round," is 7.85m, a score also reflected on the scoreboard. A brief shot shows Aleksandr Menkov, an athlete from ANA in a blue and pink Nike singlet, calmly approaching the takeoff area.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of the densely packed Stadium Crowd, creating an electric atmosphere under the bright lights of the IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017. Commentators discuss the high standards being set, noting that even a season's best of 8.19m might not be enough to stay in the competition. Emiliano Lasa, the Uruguayan long jumper in his white singlet, begins his powerful run-up on the red track. As he accelerates towards the Takeoff Board and leaps, a distinct landing sound is heard, followed by a commentator exclaiming, "Oh, that's a good jump though!" The White Flag Official raises a white flag, confirming a valid jump, which the commentator notes is "around about his best for the season." The commentator confirms Lasa's previous best was 8.06m, praising this jump as "a good jump from this man," and elaborating on the exceptionally high quality of the long jump final, which has "gone way past expectations." As Lasa walks away, visibly satisfied, the commentator anticipates, "Saving the best for last, definitely." The Scoreboard then updates, and the commentator announces, "Oh, 8.11," noting it's a "slight improvement" but unfortunately only places him in ninth. Lasa claps to the crowd, acknowledging their presence, as the segment concludes with him walking confidently, his performance highlighted against the backdrop of TOYOTA, SEIKO, TDK, and Newham London banners, under the stadium's grand roof structure.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a red hoodie, likely a coach, gesturing animatedly while interacting with another man in a teal jacket against a blue backdrop with partial 'UK' text. Simultaneously, a male commentator with a British accent discusses the competition rules, noting that only the first six or eight athletes advance to the next three jumps. The scene then transitions to a wide-angle view of the Olympic Stadium at night, its illuminated roof structure and packed stands creating a vibrant atmosphere, with the track and field area brightly lit. Another commentator mentions a 'glimpse of Manyonga' looking relaxed, before the first commentator announces Yuhao Shi of China, describing him as 'the last of the long jumpers in this third round.' Interestingly, the commentator notes that 'his coach is pleased with his performance,' even with the foul. The sequence concludes with Shi walking away from the pit, appearing composed, as the stadium lights continue to illuminate the scene, and the crowd's applause and general ambiance persist.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Ruswahl Samaai of South Africa, in his green and yellow singlet, recovering in the sandpit after a jump, as commentators discuss South Africa's medal hopes. The scoreboard highlights his 8.27m jump, which a commentator exclaims is the 'same distance as Menkov,' noting Samaai's better jump and Menkov's previous fouls, moving Samaai into third place. The focus then shifts to Aleksandr Menkov of ANA, in a blue and pink Nike singlet, preparing for his jump, with commentators remarking he's 'seen that medal slip away' and is in 'fourth place.' Menkov executes a powerful run-up and leaps into the sandpit, accompanied by crowd anticipation, but an official immediately raises a red flag, confirmed by the commentator's 'red flag goes up anyway,' indicating a foul. Menkov walks away composed, as the commentator notes his disappointment and continued fourth place. Next, Jarrion Lawson of the USA, in a blue Nike singlet, prepares with intense focus, introduced by the commentator as 'Lawson, Round five.' He performs a strong jump, landing with a splash, and the scoreboard displays his 8.43m (SB) jump, securing his second-place position, which the commentator confirms, highlighting the 'preciously close' competition with 'centimeters separating everybody.' The stadium crowd is seen applauding and cheering throughout, adding to the high-stakes atmosphere, with prominent banners for 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' and sponsors like 'TOYOTA', 'SEIKO', and 'TDK' visible.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with commentators anticipating a "massive jump" from Luvo Manyonga, noting he's "been looking so, so good." Manyonga, in his green and yellow South Africa singlet, executes a powerful run-up and leap into the sandpit. Initially, a commentator expresses disappointment, saying, "Oh no, not as good as he thought he was going to do." However, a female official quickly raises a white flag, an action confirmed by the commentator, indicating a valid attempt. The scoreboard then displays his result of 8.48 meters, which the commentator confirms "won't improve on his 8.48 in the second round," but quickly adds, "Doesn't matter, still there in first place at the moment." The camera captures the excited, densely packed stadium crowd, audibly cheering, including close-ups of a man in a black polo shirt with a lanyard and another in a red 'ARKANSAS' hoodie, both visibly engaged. The commentator highlights the historical significance: "if he wins, he'll be the first South African champion in his event for Africa." A close-up of the takeoff board shows Manyonga’s foot just before takeoff, with a digital overlay indicating a +0.4 wind reading, while the commentator notes he was "a bit back from that plasticine." His coach is then shown, with the commentator speculating, "I think he knows there's a big jump there somewhere." The scene transitions to a wide aerial shot of the illuminated Olympic Park and the stadium at night, showcasing digital banners for 'TOYOTA', 'SEIKO', and 'TDK', along with the 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' banner. The scoreboard updates to show Manyonga (8.48m), Lawson (8.43m), and Samaai (8.27m) in the top three. The segment concludes with Manyonga walking away, looking focused and composed, as the crowd continues to cheer, and the commentator emphasizes he's "closing in on the world title," reflecting on his "colorful" life story. The environment is electric with bright stadium lights and prominent 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' banners.

segments.integrated_caption: The video captures the triumphant aftermath of Luvo Manyonga’s gold medal-winning long jump at the IAAF World Championships London 2017. After landing in the sandpit with a powerful splash, Manyonga rises and begins celebrating his victory, his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet with 'MANYONGA' and 'LONDON 2017' on the bib clearly visible. An unseen commentator exclaims, "Oh, wow, wonderful, wonderful competition and a great gold medal for South Africa here," noting Manyonga "came in as the favorite and he really did produce the goods." The on-screen graphic confirms Manyonga’s win with a jump of 8.48m, labeled 'WINNER LONG JUMP MEN' and 'GOLD'. He is joined by his fellow South African teammate Ruswahl Samaai, also in a matching green and yellow singlet with 'SAMAAL' on his bib. The two athletes embrace warmly, sharing a moment of national pride as the commentator highlights "gold and bronze for South Africa" and "their first two medals," describing them as "two teammates and the two good friends." They are surrounded by photographers and media personnel, including a man in a black vest and another in a purple shirt, actively capturing the historic moment. The athletes then walk along the track, waving to the massive, cheering crowd in the brightly lit stadium, with Manyonga draped in the South African flag. They high-five each other and gesture to the audience, their expressions filled with joy and relief. Trackside banners for 'TOYOTA' and 'TDK' are visible, along with the 'IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017' signage. The commentator then reflects on Manyonga's inspiring journey, stating, "Manyonga's story shows you are never too late to turn your life around," detailing how he "made some bad decisions," "spent some time in jail," but "went back to the drawing board" and "back to athletics, what he loved," after previously finishing fifth in Daegu.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an elated Luvo Manyonga, the South African long jumper, celebrating his gold medal victory at the IAAF World Championships London 2017. Draped in the South African flag, he waves it triumphantly on the red track, his green and yellow 'SOUTH AFRICA' singlet visible, as a commentator describes how he "drove him" and is now "raising the flag for the Rainbow Nation." The commentator hails him as the "Olympic silver medalist" who is now the "World Champion," emphasizing "what a story of redemption." Manyonga, with a broad smile, high-fives teammate Ruswahl Samaai, who is also in a matching singlet. The on-screen graphic confirms his win with a jump of 8.48m, marked as 'GOLD'. The scene transitions to a wide-angle view of the packed stadium, where the full scoreboard for the Long Jump Men's final is displayed. The commentator provides "confirmation of that wonderful long jump," detailing "Luvo Manyonga with the gold medal, 8.48." He then notes "Jarrion Lawson tried his best as he could with a season's best 8.44 and silver medal," and "Ruswahl Samaai getting that all-important bronze medal for South Africa." The scoreboard lists Manyonga in first place with 8.48m, followed by Jarrion Lawson (USA) with 8.44m (SB), and Ruswahl Samaai (RSA) with 8.32m. The stadium crowd, under bright lights, erupts in loud cheers and applause, with many spectators recording the moment on their phones, creating a vibrant and celebratory atmosphere. Banners for IAAF World Championships LONDON 2017, TOYOTA, and SEIKO are visible, underscoring the professional event.

The scoreboard remains on screen for the majority of the segment, emphasizing the final standings and the significance of Manyonga’s victory.
